explanation.text: The article gives many examples: fish fathers use their mouths, frog fathers use their throats, penguins use their feet, and foxes hide food. These examples support the opening because they show different ways animal fathers care for their young.

A Darwin's frog keeps its babies inside its throat, while an Emperor penguin holds an egg on his feet beneath his belly skin. Both fathers therefore use parts of their bodies to protect their young from harm.
The penguin places the egg on his feet and covers it with a "flap" of thick belly skin to keep it warm. The word means a loose covering because the skin lies over and protects the egg.
The father fox brings food and then buries extra meat near the den under twigs. Hiding the meat is special because it teaches his kits to search for and find food themselves.

The table adds seahorses, owls, rheas, and jacanas and lists each father's unusual caregiving action. It supports the article by giving more examples of animal fathers protecting, feeding, or raising babies.

Every section describes a different animal father caring for young through an unusual behavior, from carrying babies in a throat to teaching kits to hunt. Together, these examples support the broad main idea that animal fathers have remarkable ways of helping their young.
The author says he considered the magician story funny and good enough to become a book. That belief explains why he sent it to a Random House editor: he thought other people would enjoy reading it.

Editors rejected the author's first three book attempts with his brother, but an editor accepted the fourth. Their repeated submissions support his advice because continued effort eventually led to publication.
The author has just explained that his story appeared in the Oakland Tribune's "Kids' Corner." Calling himself a "published writer" means his written work was printed where the public could read it.

Paragraph 6 moves from the author's newspaper story in third grade to rejected books in college, a fourth accepted attempt, and publication one year later. The structure is chronological because those details appear in the order the events occurred.

The author says he still feels a thrill when holding a new book or seeing a story in print, and he celebrates his Mulan screen credit. These examples show that he continues to enjoy seeing his work published and adapted.

The article traces years of writing, rejection, repeated effort, and eventual publication, then encourages young writers to persist. "However long it takes, there are great rewards" best supports that main idea because it joins perseverance with its result.
